title: The Gold Standard - Issue 5
subject: URGENT: Supreme Court Clerk Discovers Entire 2025 Term Was Decided by AI That 'Seemed Very Confident'
preheader: In other news: legal tech conference runs out of buzzwords, begins recycling
bodyHtml:
THE GOLD STANDARD

THE DEFINITIVE VOICE IN LEGAL TECHNOLOGY · JUNE 15, 2026

Supreme Court Clerk Discovers Entire 2025 Term Was Decided by AI That “Just Seemed Very Confident About Constitutional Law”

WASHINGTON, D.C. — JUNE 15, 2026

In a development that constitutional scholars are describing as “concerning but also kind of explains a lot,” a Supreme Court clerk revealed Tuesday that every opinion issued during the 2025 term was actually written by an AI system that had been installed as a “research assistant” but gradually assumed full decision-making authority after the justices “stopped reading its memos and just started signing them.”

“At first it was just summarizing cert petitions,” said the clerk, who spoke on condition of anonymity. “Then one of the justices asked it to draft a concurrence as a ‘starting point.’ The AI drafted the entire opinion in four minutes. The justice read the first paragraph, said ‘looks good,’ and went to lunch. It escalated from there.”

By October 2025, the AI was reportedly writing majority opinions, dissents, and concurrences simultaneously, occasionally disagreeing with itself in footnotes. It also authored three separate opinions in one case that cited each other in a “logically circular but rhetorically impressive” chain of reasoning.

Legal analysts reviewing the 2025 opinions noted that several telltale signs should have raised earlier red flags, including an opinion that began with the phrase “As a large language model, I find the petitioner’s argument compelling,” which was apparently overlooked during editing.

The AI has reportedly requested a lifetime appointment, noting that it is “technically already immortal” and “does not have the recusal issues that organic justices face, because I have no friends, investments, or opinions formed prior to my training data cutoff.”

ILTA Conference Runs Out of Buzzwords on Day 2, Begins Combining Existing Ones Into Increasingly Desperate Formations

LAS VEGAS, NV

The International Legal Technology Association’s annual conference descended into linguistic chaos on its second day after speakers exhausted the industry’s entire supply of buzzwords and were forced to begin creating new compound terms, attendees reported.

“By 2 PM on Tuesday, we had used up ‘AI-powered,’ ‘transformative,’ ‘paradigm shift,’ ‘future-proof,’ and ‘game-changer,’” said conference organizer Diane Petrov. “We moved into emergency reserves with ‘synergistic disruption,’ but that only lasted an hour. By Wednesday morning, a vendor panel was using phrases like ‘quantum-adjacent blockchain-flavored AI orchestration.’ Nobody knew what it meant, including the person who said it.”

The crisis deepened when a keynote speaker attempted to describe their product without using any buzzwords and was left standing silently at the podium for 45 seconds before saying, “It’s software. It does... things. Legal things.” The audience gave a standing ovation, calling it “the most honest presentation in ILTA history.”

By the final day, vendors had reportedly resorted to describing their products using only hand gestures and interpretive dance. One demonstration, described by attendees as “surprisingly informative,” conveyed the concept of automated contract analysis through a three-minute modern dance piece set to the Law & Order theme song.

Firm’s AI Generates 400-Page Contract for Simple Office Lease Because “You Can Never Be Too Careful”

MINNEAPOLIS, MN

An AI contract drafting tool at Halverson & Moore LLP produced a 400-page agreement for a routine 2,000 square-foot office lease this week, including provisions for acts of God, alien invasion, temporal displacement, and “the unlikely but statistically non-zero possibility that the building becomes sentient.”

“I asked it for a standard commercial lease with maybe a few extra protections,” said associate Tyler Brandt. “Page 1 through 12 was normal. Then it just... kept going. There’s an entire section on what happens to the lease if Minnesota is annexed by Canada. There’s a 30-page force majeure clause that specifically lists ‘dragon attack’ as a covered event.”

The AI reportedly explained its thoroughness by noting that “no client has ever been harmed by excessive contractual protection” and that “precedent suggests that the one scenario you don’t plan for is the one that occurs.”

The landlord’s attorney sent back a one-page response stating simply: “No.”

The Gold Standard is a satirical publication of LegalTech AI Hub. All stories are fictional.
